Löfgren syndrome (bilateral hilar LAD, erythema nodosum, polyarthralgia, and fever) is a classic acute presentation. Elevated serum ACE is found in approximately 75% of untreated patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hodgkin lymphoma can cause hilar lymphadenopathy but does not typically present with erythema nodosum, polyarthralgia, and elevated ACE levels as a constellation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tuberculosis can cause hilar lymphadenopathy and granulomas, but the granulomas are caseating. TB does not typically present with erythema nodosum and bilateral symmetric hilar LAD in a young woman.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis presents with dyspnea and cough but is associated with antigen exposure and does not cause bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy or erythema nodosum.
whyWrongByChoice.E: SLE can cause pulmonary disease but typically presents with pleuritis, malar rash, and joint symptoms, not bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with erythema nodosum.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman undergoes bronchoscopy with transbronchial biopsy for evaluation of bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy found on chest radiograph. The biopsy reveals well-formed granulomas composed of epithelioid macrophages and multinucleated giant cells without central necrosis. Acid-fast and fungal stains are negative. Which of the following best describes this histologic finding?

explanation.summary: The hallmark histologic finding of sarcoidosis is noncaseating granulomas — organized collections of epithelioid macrophages and giant cells without central necrosis. Negative acid-fast and fungal stains help exclude infectious causes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Noncaseating granulomas are the defining histopathologic feature of sarcoidosis. They consist of tightly clustered epithelioid macrophages and multinucleated giant cells surrounded by lymphocytes, without the central caseous necrosis seen in tuberculosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Caseating granulomas have a central area of amorphous necrosis (caseous necrosis) and are characteristic of tuberculosis and certain fungal infections, not sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Necrotizing vasculitis is characteristic of diseases like granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA), not sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Eosinophilic infiltrates are characteristic of conditions like eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA) or allergic diseases, not sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fibrinoid necrosis is a pattern of vascular wall damage seen in vasculitis and malignant hypertension, not in the granulomatous inflammation of sarcoidosis.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man with sarcoidosis presents with fatigue, increased thirst, and frequent urination. Serum calcium is 12.8 mg/dL (normal: 8.5–10.5 mg/dL). Which of the following best explains the mechanism of hypercalcemia in this patient?

explanation.summary: In sarcoidosis, activated macrophages within granulomas express 1-alpha-hydroxylase, which converts 25-hydroxyvitamin D to active 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (calcitriol). This increases intestinal calcium absorption, bone resorption, and renal calcium reabsorption, leading to hypercalcemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Granulomas in sarcoidosis produce calcitriol (1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D) via macrophage-derived 1-alpha-hydroxylase. This ectopic vitamin D production increases gut calcium absorption and bone calcium release, causing hypercalcemia in 10–20% of sarcoidosis patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Primary hyperparathyroidism from a parathyroid adenoma is a common cause of hypercalcemia in the general population but is not the mechanism in sarcoidosis-related hypercalcemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Decreased renal calcium excretion can contribute to hypercalcemia in some conditions, but in sarcoidosis the primary mechanism is excess calcitriol production. Renal calcium excretion is actually increased, sometimes leading to calcium oxalate stones.
whyWrongByChoice.E: PTHrP secretion is a mechanism of humoral hypercalcemia of malignancy, not sarcoidosis. Granulomas do not produce PTHrP.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Osteolytic metastases cause hypercalcemia via local bone destruction. This patient has sarcoidosis, not a malignancy with bone metastases.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with known sarcoidosis undergoes pulmonary function testing. Which of the following patterns is most likely?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased FVC with increased FEV1/FVC ratio

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased FEV1 with decreased FEV1/FVC ratio

explanation.summary: Pulmonary sarcoidosis causes a restrictive pattern on PFTs, characterized by decreased lung volumes (FVC, TLC) with a normal or increased FEV1/FVC ratio. The diffusing capacity (DLCO) is also typically decreased.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sarcoidosis causes diffuse interstitial inflammation and granuloma formation that restricts lung expansion. This results in decreased FVC and TLC, with a proportional decrease in FEV1 so that the FEV1/FVC ratio is preserved or increased — the hallmark of a restrictive pattern.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A decreased FEV1/FVC ratio indicates an obstructive pattern (e.g., COPD, asthma). Sarcoidosis causes restrictive disease with a preserved or increased ratio.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While DLCO may be decreased in sarcoidosis, FVC is also characteristically reduced. An isolated decrease in DLCO with normal volumes suggests early pulmonary vascular disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: In restrictive disease, TLC is decreased, not increased. Increased TLC is seen in obstructive diseases due to air trapping.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An increased FEV1 is not expected in sarcoidosis. Both FEV1 and FVC decrease proportionally in restrictive disease.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with biopsy-confirmed sarcoidosis is found to have bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and diffuse reticulonodular infiltrates on chest radiograph. She has no parenchymal fibrosis. Which radiographic stage of sarcoidosis does this represent?

explanation.summary: Sarcoidosis staging is based on chest radiograph findings. Stage II is defined by bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with pulmonary infiltrates but without fibrosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Stage II sarcoidosis is characterized by both bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and parenchymal infiltrates on chest radiograph. This distinguishes it from Stage I (hilar LAD alone), Stage III (infiltrates without LAD), and Stage IV (fibrosis).

whyWrongByChoice.C: Stage III shows pulmonary infiltrates without hilar lymphadenopathy. This patient still has hilar LAD.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Stage IV is characterized by pulmonary fibrosis with volume loss and irreversible changes. This patient has no fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Stage 0 refers to a normal chest radiograph in a patient with extrapulmonary sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Stage I shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy alone, without pulmonary parenchymal infiltrates.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with known sarcoidosis presents with acute onset of eye pain, redness, and photophobia in her left eye. Slit-lamp examination reveals inflammatory cells in the anterior chamber. Which of the following is the most likely ocular complication?

explanation.summary: Anterior uveitis is the most common ocular manifestation of sarcoidosis, occurring in approximately 25% of patients. It presents with eye pain, redness, and photophobia, with inflammatory cells visible in the anterior chamber on slit-lamp examination.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sarcoidosis commonly involves the eyes, with anterior uveitis being the most frequent manifestation. The granulomatous inflammation affects the iris and ciliary body, producing pain at the limbus, redness, photophobia, and anterior chamber cells on slit-lamp exam.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Central retinal vein occlusion causes painless vision loss with retinal hemorrhages. It is not a typical manifestation of sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Optic neuritis can occur in sarcoidosis (neurosarcoidosis) but presents with vision loss and pain on eye movement, not anterior chamber cells.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Conjunctivitis causes eye redness and discharge but does not produce anterior chamber inflammation on slit-lamp examination.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Acute angle-closure glaucoma presents with severe eye pain, halos, and a mid-dilated pupil. It is caused by obstruction of aqueous outflow, not granulomatous inflammation.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man presents with bilateral facial weakness, dry cough, and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on chest radiograph. Transbronchial biopsy reveals noncaseating granulomas. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his facial weakness?

explanation.summary: Neurosarcoidosis can cause cranial neuropathies, with facial nerve (CN VII) palsy being the most common neurologic manifestation. Bilateral facial palsy in a patient with confirmed sarcoidosis strongly suggests granulomatous infiltration of the facial nerves.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sarcoidosis can affect the nervous system (neurosarcoidosis), with facial nerve palsy being the most common cranial neuropathy. Bilateral involvement is characteristic and should raise suspicion for sarcoidosis rather than idiopathic Bell palsy, which is typically unilateral.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bell palsy (idiopathic facial nerve palsy) is typically unilateral. Bilateral facial palsy in the setting of systemic granulomatous disease points to neurosarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Myasthenia gravis causes fluctuating weakness of ocular and bulbar muscles. Isolated bilateral facial weakness with hilar LAD and granulomas is not consistent with this diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Guillain-Barré syndrome can cause bilateral facial weakness but also causes ascending limb weakness and areflexia. The presence of noncaseating granulomas and hilar LAD makes sarcoidosis the primary diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A cerebrovascular accident causing bilateral facial weakness would be extremely rare and would involve upper motor neuron signs, not the lower motor neuron pattern seen here.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with sarcoidosis confirmed 2 years ago presents with worsening dyspnea and a new holosystolic murmur best heard at the apex. Echocardiography reveals reduced left ventricular ejection fraction and mitral regurgitation. Which of the following best explains her cardiac findings?

explanation.summary: Cardiac sarcoidosis involves granulomatous infiltration of the myocardium, which can cause arrhythmias, conduction abnormalities, heart failure, and valvular dysfunction. Myocardial involvement is a serious complication and one of the leading causes of death in sarcoidosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cardiac sarcoidosis results from noncaseating granuloma formation within the myocardium. This infiltration can damage the conduction system, cause cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction, and lead to mitral regurgitation from papillary muscle or valve involvement.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Coronary artery atherosclerosis causes ischemic cardiomyopathy, but in a patient with established sarcoidosis and no mention of coronary risk factors, cardiac sarcoidosis is the more likely etiology.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Infective endocarditis presents with fever, new murmur, and vegetations on echocardiography. The clinical context of known sarcoidosis makes granulomatous infiltration more likely.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is a genetic condition causing asymmetric septal hypertrophy, not a complication of sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cardiac amyloidosis causes restrictive cardiomyopathy with diastolic dysfunction, typically presenting with thickened ventricular walls. This patient has dilated cardiomyopathy with reduced EF.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with biopsy-confirmed sarcoidosis has worsening dyspnea, bilateral pulmonary infiltrates, and an FVC of 55% predicted. She is started on a medication and returns 6 weeks later with improved symptoms and an FVC of 72% predicted. Which of the following medications was most likely initiated?

explanation.summary: Oral corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone) are the first-line treatment for symptomatic pulmonary sarcoidosis. An initial 4–6 week course is used to assess response, which includes improvement in symptoms, PFTs, and chest radiograph findings.
explanation.whyCorrect: Prednisone is the first-line drug for symptomatic sarcoidosis with significant pulmonary involvement. It is typically given for 4–6 weeks initially, with response assessed by symptom improvement, rising FVC, and clearing of radiographic infiltrates.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Azithromycin is an antibiotic with no role in treating sarcoidosis. Sarcoidosis is not an infectious disease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Infliximab (anti-TNF-alpha) is reserved for refractory sarcoidosis that does not respond to corticosteroids and conventional immunosuppressants.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ibuprofen is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug that does not have sufficient immunosuppressive activity to treat significant pulmonary sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Methotrexate is a second-line immunosuppressant used when corticosteroids fail or as a steroid-sparing agent. It is not the initial treatment.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with violaceous, indurated plaques on her nose, cheeks, and ears that have been present for several months. She also has bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on chest radiograph. Biopsy of a skin lesion reveals noncaseating granulomas. Which of the following skin findings is described?

explanation.summary: Lupus pernio is a chronic, disfiguring skin manifestation of sarcoidosis characterized by violaceous, indurated plaques on the nose, cheeks, ears, and lips. It indicates chronic, severe sarcoidosis and is distinct from erythema nodosum.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lupus pernio consists of violaceous, indurated plaques and nodules on the nose, cheeks, ears, and lips. It is a specific cutaneous manifestation of sarcoidosis that contains noncaseating granulomas on biopsy and is associated with chronic, more severe systemic disease.

whyWrongByChoice.C: A malar rash (butterfly rash) is a flat, erythematous rash over the cheeks and nose bridge that spares the nasolabial folds, seen in SLE. It is not indurated or violaceous.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Discoid lupus causes well-demarcated, scaly, erythematous plaques with follicular plugging, typically on the face and scalp. Biopsy would show interface dermatitis, not granulomas.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pyoderma gangrenosum is an ulcerative skin condition associated with inflammatory bowel disease, not sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Erythema nodosum presents as tender, red nodules on the shins (subcutaneous fat inflammation). It is a nonspecific reactive process and does not contain granulomas on biopsy.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old African American woman is found to have bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on a pre-employment chest radiograph. She is completely asymptomatic with a normal physical examination. Bronchoscopy with transbronchial biopsy confirms noncaseating granulomas. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

explanation.summary: Asymptomatic sarcoidosis (Stage I with bilateral hilar LAD only) has a high rate of spontaneous remission and does not require drug therapy. Observation with periodic monitoring is the appropriate management.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with minimal or no symptoms from sarcoidosis can be observed without drug therapy, as many experience spontaneous remission. Corticosteroids are reserved for patients with significant symptoms, progressive disease, or organ-threatening involvement.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Prednisone is indicated for symptomatic or progressive sarcoidosis, not for asymptomatic patients with hilar LAD alone.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cyclophosphamide is used for severe, refractory autoimmune diseases. It has significant toxicity and is not indicated for asymptomatic sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lung transplant evaluation is reserved for end-stage pulmonary sarcoidosis (Stage IV) with respiratory failure, not early asymptomatic disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Methotrexate is a second-line agent used when corticosteroids fail. It would not be initiated in an asymptomatic patient.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with long-standing sarcoidosis presents with progressive dyspnea and lower extremity edema. Chest radiograph shows upper lobe fibrosis with volume loss and honeycombing. Echocardiography reveals elevated right ventricular systolic pressure. Which of the following best explains the right heart dysfunction?

explanation.summary: Stage IV sarcoidosis with pulmonary fibrosis leads to destruction of the pulmonary vascular bed, increasing pulmonary vascular resistance. This causes pulmonary hypertension and right heart failure (cor pulmonale).
explanation.whyCorrect: Advanced pulmonary sarcoidosis with fibrosis (Stage IV) destroys alveolar-capillary units and distorts pulmonary vasculature, increasing pulmonary vascular resistance. The resulting pulmonary hypertension causes right ventricular strain, elevated RVSP, and eventually cor pulmonale with peripheral edema.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Left ventricular systolic failure can cause secondary pulmonary hypertension, but the primary pathology here is pulmonary fibrosis causing right heart strain, not left heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonary embolism can cause acute right heart strain but does not explain the chronic progressive dyspnea and fibrotic changes on chest radiograph.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tricuspid valve endocarditis would present with fever, septic emboli, and is associated with IV drug use, not pulmonary fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Constrictive pericarditis causes equal elevation of diastolic pressures in all chambers and does not explain elevated RVSP from pulmonary fibrosis.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with recently diagnosed sarcoidosis undergoes bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL). Which of the following BAL findings is most characteristic of this disease?

explanation.summary: Bronchoalveolar lavage in sarcoidosis characteristically shows lymphocytic alveolitis with an elevated CD4:CD8 T-cell ratio, reflecting the T-helper cell–mediated granulomatous immune response.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sarcoidosis is driven by CD4+ T-helper cell activation and granuloma formation. BAL in sarcoidosis typically shows lymphocytosis with an elevated CD4:CD8 ratio, often greater than 3.5:1, reflecting the predominance of activated CD4+ T cells in the alveolar compartment.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Neutrophilia on BAL is more characteristic of bacterial infections, ARDS, or idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, not sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Elevated eosinophils on BAL suggest eosinophilic pneumonia or EGPA, not sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Malignant cells on BAL indicate a pulmonary malignancy, which is not a feature of sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A decreased CD4:CD8 ratio (with predominant CD8+ cells) is seen in hypersensitivity pneumonitis, not sarcoidosis.

questions.stem: A 37-year-old man with sarcoidosis develops recurrent episodes of flank pain. Urinalysis shows microscopic hematuria. A CT scan of the abdomen reveals a 6-mm stone in the left ureter. Which of the following is the most likely composition of this renal stone?

explanation.summary: Sarcoidosis causes hypercalcemia through granuloma production of calcitriol, leading to increased renal calcium excretion (hypercalciuria). This excess calcium in the urine precipitates as calcium oxalate stones.
explanation.whyCorrect: Granulomas in sarcoidosis produce 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D, causing hypercalcemia and hypercalciuria. The excess urinary calcium forms calcium oxalate stones, which are the most common type of kidney stone in sarcoidosis-related nephrolithiasis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Uric acid stones are associated with gout, myeloproliferative disorders, and acidic urine, not with the hypercalcemia of sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Struvite (magnesium ammonium phosphate) stones form in the setting of urease-producing bacterial infections causing alkaline urine.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While calcium phosphate stones can occur with hypercalcemia, calcium oxalate stones are more common in sarcoidosis-related hypercalciuria.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Cystine stones result from cystinuria, a genetic disorder of amino acid transport, unrelated to sarcoidosis.

questions.stem: A pathologist examining a lung biopsy from a patient with sarcoidosis identifies multinucleated giant cells containing stellate, eosinophilic inclusions with filamentous radiating arms. Which of the following structures is being described?

explanation.summary: Asteroid bodies are stellate (star-shaped) eosinophilic inclusions with filamentous radiating arms found within the cytoplasm of multinucleated giant cells in sarcoidosis. Though not pathognomonic, they are a characteristic histologic finding.
explanation.whyCorrect: Asteroid bodies are star-shaped inclusions found within multinucleated giant cells in granulomas. They consist of a stellate arrangement of filamentous arms and are commonly seen in sarcoidosis, although they can also be found in other granulomatous diseases.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Schaumann bodies are calcium and protein inclusions (laminated concretions) within giant cells. They do not have the stellate, filamentous appearance described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ferruginous bodies are iron-coated asbestos fibers found in the lungs of patients with asbestos exposure, not in sarcoidosis granulomas.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Langhans giant cells are multinucleated giant cells with nuclei arranged in a horseshoe pattern at the cell periphery. They are cells, not intracellular inclusions.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Councilman bodies are eosinophilic apoptotic hepatocytes seen in viral hepatitis, unrelated to sarcoidosis.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old woman presents with fever, bilateral ankle pain, tender erythematous nodules on both shins, and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on chest radiograph. Which of the following cytokines plays a key role in recruiting and activating macrophages in the formation of granulomas in this patient's disease?

explanation.summary: TNF-alpha is a key pro-inflammatory cytokine in sarcoidosis pathogenesis. It is released by activated T cells and macrophages and plays a central role in recruiting and activating macrophages that form the noncaseating granulomas characteristic of sarcoidosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: In sarcoidosis, T-helper cells release cytokines including TNF-alpha, which recruits and activates macrophages. These macrophages evolve into epithelioid cells and fuse into multinucleated giant cells, forming the characteristic noncaseating granulomas.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Interleukin-4 promotes B-cell class switching to IgE and Th2 differentiation. It is involved in allergic responses, not granuloma formation in sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Interleukin-5 promotes eosinophil activation and is key in eosinophilic diseases like EGPA, not in the granulomatous inflammation of sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Interleukin-10 is an anti-inflammatory cytokine that suppresses immune responses. It does not drive granuloma formation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Interleukin-13 promotes fibrosis and mucus production and is involved in asthma pathophysiology, not in granulomatous inflammation.

questions.stem: A 33-year-old woman with sarcoidosis is being treated with prednisone for progressive pulmonary disease. After 6 months, she develops cushingoid facies and hyperglycemia. Her physician wants to add a steroid-sparing agent. Which of the following is the most appropriate second-line drug?

explanation.summary: Methotrexate is the most commonly used second-line immunosuppressant for sarcoidosis when corticosteroids cause intolerable side effects or are insufficient. It allows dose reduction of corticosteroids while maintaining disease control.
explanation.whyCorrect: When corticosteroids alone are insufficient or cause significant adverse effects, methotrexate is the preferred steroid-sparing agent for pulmonary sarcoidosis. It is an immunosuppressant that helps control granulomatous inflammation while allowing reduction of corticosteroid doses.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Etanercept (anti-TNF) has shown limited efficacy in sarcoidosis trials. Infliximab is the preferred anti-TNF agent for refractory disease, not etanercept.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Cyclophosphamide has significant toxicity (hemorrhagic cystitis, infertility) and is reserved for severe, life-threatening autoimmune diseases, not as a routine steroid-sparing agent.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hydroxychloroquine is sometimes used for cutaneous or joint sarcoidosis but is not the standard second-line agent for progressive pulmonary disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Azathioprine can be used as a steroid-sparing agent but is generally considered less well-established than methotrexate for sarcoidosis.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old man presents with hepatomegaly. Liver biopsy reveals noncaseating granulomas. He also has bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on chest radiograph. Serum ACE level is elevated, and serum calcium is normal. Which of the following organs is most commonly involved in sarcoidosis in addition to the lungs?

explanation.summary: While sarcoidosis can affect nearly any organ, the skin and eyes are the most common extrapulmonary sites of involvement, each affected in approximately 25% of patients. Skin manifestations include erythema nodosum and lupus pernio; ocular manifestations include uveitis.
explanation.whyCorrect: After the lungs, the skin and eyes are the most frequently involved organs in sarcoidosis. Skin involvement (erythema nodosum, lupus pernio, subcutaneous nodules) and ocular involvement (anterior uveitis, lacrimal gland inflammation) each occur in about 25% of patients.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Renal involvement in sarcoidosis is uncommon and usually manifests as nephrolithiasis from hypercalciuria or rarely granulomatous interstitial nephritis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Liver and spleen involvement can occur but is less common than skin and eye involvement. Hepatic granulomas may be found incidentally, as in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pancreatic involvement in sarcoidosis is rare.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Bone marrow involvement in sarcoidosis is uncommon and not among the most frequently affected extrapulmonary sites.

questions.stem: A 31-year-old woman presents with excessive thirst and polyuria. She was diagnosed with pulmonary sarcoidosis 1 year ago. Serum calcium is normal. Serum sodium is 148 mEq/L, and urine osmolality is low despite fluid restriction. MRI of the brain shows enhancement of the hypothalamic-pituitary region. Which of the following best explains her symptoms?

explanation.summary: Neurosarcoidosis can involve the hypothalamus and pituitary gland, causing central diabetes insipidus. The MRI enhancement of the hypothalamic-pituitary region with polyuria and dilute urine in a patient with known sarcoidosis confirms granulomatous infiltration of the hypothalamus.
explanation.whyCorrect: Neurosarcoidosis can affect the hypothalamus and pituitary gland, disrupting ADH production or release and causing central diabetes insipidus. This presents with polyuria, polydipsia, dilute urine, and hypernatremia. The MRI findings of hypothalamic-pituitary enhancement confirm the diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Corticosteroid-induced diabetes mellitus would cause hyperglycemia and osmotic diuresis, not the dilute urine with low osmolality and MRI enhancement seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Primary polydipsia would cause dilute urine but also dilutional hyponatremia, not hypernatremia. It would not explain the MRI findings.
whyWrongByChoice.E: SIADH causes water retention, hyponatremia, and concentrated urine — the opposite of this patient's presentation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypercalcemia can cause nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, but this patient's calcium is normal. The MRI findings point to central, not nephrogenic, diabetes insipidus.

questions.stem: A 36-year-old woman is evaluated for sarcoidosis. Her chest radiograph shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy without parenchymal infiltrates. She has mild cough but no dyspnea. PFTs are normal. Serum ACE is elevated. Which of the following is the single best confirmatory test?

explanation.summary: There is no single gold standard diagnostic test for sarcoidosis, but tissue biopsy demonstrating noncaseating granulomas is required for definitive diagnosis. Bronchoscopy with transbronchial biopsy is the preferred method for obtaining lung tissue.
explanation.whyCorrect: A diagnosis of sarcoidosis requires demonstration of noncaseating granulomas on tissue biopsy, along with compatible clinical and radiographic features and exclusion of other causes. Bronchoscopy with transbronchial biopsy is the most commonly performed procedure to obtain diagnostic tissue.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Serum ACE is elevated in about 75% of untreated patients but has poor sensitivity and specificity. It cannot confirm the diagnosis alone.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Serum calcium may be elevated in 10–20% of patients but is neither sensitive nor specific for sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A tuberculin skin test helps exclude tuberculosis (which causes caseating granulomas). Sarcoidosis patients often have anergy, making this test unreliable for confirming sarcoidosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: HRCT can confirm and detail radiographic findings but cannot provide a tissue diagnosis. It does not replace biopsy for confirmation.

questions.stem: A 39-year-old man presents with cough and dyspnea. Chest radiograph shows pulmonary infiltrates but no hilar lymphadenopathy. Transbronchial biopsy reveals noncaseating granulomas with minimal surrounding inflammation. Stains for organisms are negative. Which of the following conditions is most likely?

explanation.summary: Stage III sarcoidosis shows pulmonary infiltrates without hilar lymphadenopathy on chest radiograph. The key distinguishing feature of sarcoidosis granulomas is the absence of intense surrounding inflammation, unlike hypersensitivity pneumonitis and drug-induced hypersensitivity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Noncaseating granulomas with minimal surrounding inflammation, negative organism stains, and pulmonary infiltrates without hilar LAD characterize Stage III sarcoidosis. In hypersensitivity pneumonitis and drug reactions, granulomas are accompanied by intense surrounding inflammation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis can produce noncaseating granulomas, but these are accompanied by intense surrounding lymphocytic inflammation and are associated with antigen exposure history.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Tuberculosis produces caseating granulomas with central necrosis, not noncaseating granulomas.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Drug-induced hypersensitivity can produce granulomas but typically with intense surrounding inflammation, eosinophilic infiltrates, and a clear temporal relationship to drug exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Berylliosis mimics sarcoidosis closely but requires a history of beryllium exposure (aerospace, electronics manufacturing) and is confirmed by beryllium lymphocyte proliferation test.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman presents with dry cough and bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. She is diagnosed with sarcoidosis after transbronchial biopsy. Arterial blood gas analysis shows PaO2 of 88 mmHg and PaCO2 of 32 mmHg on room air. Which of the following best explains the blood gas findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Normal gas exchange in early disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hyperventilation from stimulation of interstitial receptors

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chronic respiratory acidosis with renal compensation

explanation.summary: In sarcoidosis, interstitial inflammation stimulates juxtacapillary (J) receptors in the lung, triggering hyperventilation. This results in mild hypocapnia (low PaCO2) with relatively preserved oxygenation, a common finding in interstitial lung diseases.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sarcoidosis involves diffuse interstitial inflammation that stimulates pulmonary J-receptors, causing a compensatory increase in respiratory rate (hyperventilation). This lowers PaCO2 below normal (hypocapnia). The PaO2 may be near-normal in early disease due to the increased ventilation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While PaO2 is near the low end of normal, the PaCO2 of 32 mmHg is below normal (35–45 mmHg), indicating hyperventilation — not normal gas exchange.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hypercalcemia does not directly stimulate hyperventilation. Metabolic compensation for hypercalcemia would not cause respiratory alkalosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Respiratory muscle weakness would cause hypoventilation with elevated PaCO2 (hypercapnia), the opposite of what is seen here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chronic respiratory acidosis would show elevated PaCO2, not the low PaCO2 seen in this patient.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents with hemoptysis and hematuria over the past 2 weeks. He has a 5-pack-year smoking history. Serum creatinine is 4.2 mg/dL (baseline 0.9). Chest radiograph shows bilateral diffuse alveolar infiltrates. Renal biopsy demonstrates linear IgG deposition along the glomerular basement membrane on immunofluorescence. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture syndrome) presents with the combination of pulmonary hemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. The pathognomonic finding is linear IgG deposition along the glomerular basement membrane on immunofluorescence.
explanation.whyCorrect: Anti-GBM disease is caused by autoantibodies against type IV collagen in basement membranes of the glomeruli and alveoli. The hallmark finding is linear IgG staining along the GBM on immunofluorescence. Smoking may expose alveolar basement membranes to autoantibodies, triggering pulmonary hemorrhage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: GPA can cause pulmonary-renal syndrome but shows necrotizing granulomas on biopsy and pauci-immune (no immunoglobulin) staining on immunofluorescence, with positive c-ANCA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: IgA nephropathy shows mesangial IgA deposits on immunofluorescence, not linear IgG along the GBM. It rarely causes pulmonary hemorrhage.
whyWrongByChoice.D: SLE can cause glomerulonephritis and rarely pulmonary hemorrhage but shows a granular ("lumpy-bumpy") pattern of immunoglobulin deposition, not linear IgG.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Microscopic polyangiitis can cause pulmonary-renal syndrome but shows pauci-immune glomerulonephritis (no immunoglobulin deposits) and is associated with p-ANCA.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with a 3-month history of bloody nasal discharge, cough with hemoptysis, and dark urine. Sinus CT shows mucosal thickening and bony erosion. Laboratory studies reveal elevated creatinine and positive c-ANCA with anti-proteinase 3 antibodies. Which of the following histologic findings would most likely be seen on lung biopsy?

explanation.summary: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) is characterized by the triad of upper airway disease, lung involvement, and renal disease. The hallmark histologic findings are necrotizing granulomas and necrotizing vasculitis, with c-ANCA (anti-PR3) positivity.
explanation.whyCorrect: GPA causes necrotizing granulomatous inflammation and small-vessel vasculitis affecting the upper and lower airways and kidneys. Lung biopsy shows necrotizing granulomas with accompanying vasculitis. c-ANCA directed against PR3 is positive in 80–95% of cases.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Noncaseating granulomas without necrosis are characteristic of sarcoidosis, not GPA. GPA granulomas are necrotizing.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Eosinophilic tissue infiltration is the hallmark of EGPA (Churg-Strauss), not GPA. EGPA is associated with asthma and p-ANCA, not c-ANCA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Honeycombing with fibrosis is characteristic of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis or end-stage interstitial lung disease, not GPA.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Linear IgG along the basement membrane is the pathognomonic finding of anti-GBM disease, not GPA.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with a 10-year history of asthma presents with new-onset sinusitis, purpuric rash on his legs, and weight loss. Complete blood count shows a WBC of 14,000/μL with 32% eosinophils. Chest radiograph reveals bilateral migratory pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: EGPA (Churg-Strauss syndrome) classically presents in a patient with asthma who develops peripheral eosinophilia (>10%), sinusitis, migratory pulmonary infiltrates, and systemic vasculitis (purpuric rash). The three sequential stages are allergic, eosinophilic, and vasculitic.
explanation.whyCorrect: EGPA is an autoimmune small-vessel vasculitis uniquely associated with asthma and allergic symptoms. This patient demonstrates the eosinophilic stage (marked eosinophilia, migratory infiltrates) with features of the vasculitic stage (purpuric rash, weight loss). Eosinophils >10% is a diagnostic criterion.

whyWrongByChoice.A: GPA causes upper airway disease and hemoptysis but is not associated with asthma, marked eosinophilia, or migratory infiltrates. It is c-ANCA positive.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ABPA causes eosinophilia and pulmonary infiltrates but occurs in the context of Aspergillus sensitization, with central bronchiectasis and elevated IgE, without vasculitic rash.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia causes peripheral eosinophilia and pulmonary infiltrates but does not cause systemic vasculitis (purpuric rash, weight loss).
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypereosinophilic syndrome causes persistent eosinophilia with organ damage but is not associated with asthma or vasculitic rash.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman with a 15-year history of rheumatoid arthritis presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion and a nonproductive cough. High-resolution CT of the chest shows bilateral ground-glass opacities and reticular markings with basal predominance. PFTs show a restrictive pattern. Which of the following is the most likely pulmonary complication?

explanation.summary: RA-associated interstitial lung disease (RA-ILD) is the most frequent and clinically significant pulmonary complication of rheumatoid arthritis. It presents with progressive dyspnea, dry cough, restrictive PFTs, and ground-glass opacities with reticular changes on HRCT.
explanation.whyCorrect: RA-ILD is the most common major pulmonary manifestation of RA. It involves inflammation and fibrosis of the lung interstitium, leading to restrictive lung disease. HRCT showing bilateral ground-glass opacities and reticular markings with basal predominance is characteristic.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pleural effusion can occur in RA but presents with unilateral or bilateral opacities at the bases on imaging, not ground-glass opacities and reticular markings.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonary embolism presents acutely with pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea but does not cause the chronic HRCT findings described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bronchiectasis can occur with RA but shows dilated airways on HRCT and causes an obstructive pattern on PFTs, not a restrictive pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pneumothorax is not a common complication of RA and presents with acute chest pain and dyspnea, not progressive symptoms.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus presents with sharp, pleuritic chest pain that worsens with deep inspiration. She has no cough or hemoptysis. Chest radiograph shows a small left-sided pleural effusion. Temperature is 38.1°C. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her symptoms?

explanation.summary: Pleuritis is the most common pulmonary manifestation of SLE. It presents with sharp pleuritic chest pain worsening with deep inspiration and may be accompanied by pleural effusion and low-grade fever.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lupus pleuritis is the most common pulmonary manifestation of SLE, characterized by inflammation of the pleural lining causing sharp, pleuritic chest pain. It can be accompanied by pleural effusion and mild fever. The absence of productive cough and the known SLE history make this the most likely diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Bacterial pneumonia would present with productive cough, high fever, and consolidation on imaging, not isolated pleuritic pain with small effusion in an SLE patient.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonary embolism can occur in SLE (especially with antiphospholipid syndrome) but typically presents with acute dyspnea and tachycardia, and imaging would show wedge-shaped infarcts, not simple effusion.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pericarditis causes chest pain that improves with leaning forward and may show a pericardial effusion, not a pleural effusion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Spontaneous pneumothorax would show absent lung markings and a visible pleural line on chest radiograph, not a pleural effusion.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis presents with progressive dyspnea over the past year. She has skin thickening extending proximal to the elbows and knees. PFTs show decreased FVC and decreased DLCO. Anti-topoisomerase-1 (anti-Scl-70) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely pulmonary complication?

explanation.summary: Interstitial lung disease is the most significant respiratory complication in diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis, particularly in patients with anti-Scl-70 antibodies. It causes restrictive lung disease with decreased FVC and DLCO.
explanation.whyCorrect: ILD is the most common and dangerous pulmonary manifestation of diffuse cutaneous scleroderma. Anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase-1) antibodies are strongly associated with ILD risk. The typical pattern is NSIP with inflammation and fibrosis causing a restrictive pattern (decreased FVC) and impaired gas exchange (decreased DLCO).

whyWrongByChoice.C: Aspiration pneumonia can occur due to esophageal dysmotility in scleroderma but would present acutely with fever and infiltrates, not progressive dyspnea with restrictive PFTs.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pleural effusions are not a common pulmonary manifestation of scleroderma.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bronchogenic carcinoma is not a recognized complication of scleroderma.
whyWrongByChoice.B: PAH is a major pulmonary complication of scleroderma but is more commonly associated with limited cutaneous scleroderma and anti-centromere antibodies, not diffuse disease with anti-Scl-70.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with hemoptysis and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. Serum anti-GBM antibody is positive by ELISA. He is started on pulse intravenous methylprednisolone and cyclophosphamide. Which of the following additional treatments is most important in managing this patient's condition?

explanation.summary: Anti-GBM disease is treated with the combination of immunosuppression (glucocorticoids plus cyclophosphamide) and plasmapheresis to rapidly remove pathogenic anti-GBM antibodies from the circulation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Plasmapheresis (plasma exchange) is a critical component of anti-GBM disease treatment. It physically removes the pathogenic anti-GBM antibodies from the blood, providing rapid reduction in antibody levels while immunosuppressive drugs work to prevent new antibody production.

whyWrongByChoice.A: IVIG is used in autoimmune diseases where immunomodulation is needed (e.g., Kawasaki disease, Guillain-Barré) but is not standard for anti-GBM disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Rituximab (anti-CD20) is used for GPA and some other vasculitides but is not first-line for anti-GBM disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mycophenolate is used as maintenance therapy in some autoimmune conditions but is not the key adjunctive therapy for acute anti-GBM disease.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Methotrexate is a steroid-sparing agent used in conditions like sarcoidosis and RA but is not part of the standard anti-GBM treatment regimen.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a history of GPA (granulomatosis with polyangiitis) presents with progressive nasal congestion and change in the appearance of his nose. On examination, there is a collapse of the nasal bridge. Which of the following best explains this finding?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Necrotizing granulomatous destruction of nasal septum

explanation.summary: Saddle nose deformity is a characteristic complication of GPA caused by necrotizing granulomatous inflammation that destroys the nasal septal cartilage, leading to collapse of the nasal bridge.
explanation.whyCorrect: GPA causes necrotizing granulomatous inflammation of the upper respiratory tract, including the nasal septum. Progressive destruction of the nasal septal cartilage leads to saddle nose deformity, a classic finding in advanced GPA.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nasal polyps are more associated with EGPA and allergic conditions. Polyps do not cause cartilage destruction and saddle nose deformity.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Mucus impaction causes nasal obstruction but does not destroy cartilage or cause structural deformity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Allergic mucosal edema causes swelling and congestion but is reversible and does not destroy cartilage.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Foreign body aspiration is an acute event typically in children and does not cause progressive nasal cartilage destruction.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (CREST syndrome) presents with worsening exertional dyspnea and fatigue. Anti-centromere antibodies are positive. Echocardiography reveals elevated estimated right ventricular systolic pressure of 55 mmHg. PFTs show a normal FVC but a significantly reduced DLCO. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) is a major complication of limited cutaneous scleroderma (CREST syndrome), particularly in patients with anti-centromere antibodies. An isolated reduction in DLCO with normal FVC and elevated RVSP is characteristic of PAH without significant ILD.
explanation.whyCorrect: Limited cutaneous scleroderma with anti-centromere antibodies carries a particularly high risk for PAH. The normal FVC with isolated DLCO reduction indicates impaired pulmonary vascular gas exchange without parenchymal restriction, and the elevated RVSP on echocardiography confirms pulmonary hypertension.

whyWrongByChoice.A: ILD would cause a restrictive pattern with decreased FVC and DLCO. This patient has normal FVC, suggesting the DLCO reduction is from vascular disease, not parenchymal fibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Left heart failure can cause secondary pulmonary hypertension but would show evidence of left-sided dysfunction on echocardiography and is not specifically associated with anti-centromere antibodies.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic thromboembolic PH is possible but is not specifically associated with scleroderma or anti-centromere antibodies. PAH is the most likely cause in this clinical context.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonary veno-occlusive disease is a rare cause of pulmonary hypertension and is not associated with scleroderma.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with anti-GBM disease has been a smoker for 15 years. His physician explains that smoking may have contributed to his pulmonary disease. Which of the following best explains why smoking increases the risk of pulmonary hemorrhage in anti-GBM disease?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Smoking damages alveolar membranes, exposing basement membrane antigens

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nicotine directly activates complement in the lungs

explanation.summary: In anti-GBM disease, the alveolar basement membrane is normally protected from circulating autoantibodies. Smoking damages the alveolar epithelium, exposing the basement membrane to anti-GBM antibodies and triggering alveolar hemorrhage.
explanation.whyCorrect: Not all patients with anti-GBM disease develop pulmonary hemorrhage because the alveolar basement membrane is normally shielded. Smoking (or other lung injury) disrupts the alveolar epithelium, making the basement membrane susceptible to anti-GBM antibody binding, complement activation, and subsequent hemorrhage.

whyWrongByChoice.C: Nicotine does not directly activate complement. The complement activation in anti-GBM disease is triggered by antibody binding to basement membrane antigens.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Smoking can contribute to pulmonary hypertension, but this is not the mechanism by which it worsens anti-GBM pulmonary disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While smoking does affect surfactant function, this is not the relevant mechanism in anti-GBM disease–related hemorrhage.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Smoking does not increase production of anti-GBM antibodies. The mechanism involves exposure of the target antigen, not increased antibody synthesis.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with Sjögren syndrome presents with a chronic dry cough. Chest radiograph is normal. She denies dyspnea, hemoptysis, or chest pain. Which of the following best explains her cough?

explanation.summary: The most frequent respiratory symptom in Sjögren syndrome is a persistent dry cough caused by xerotrachea — dryness of the trachea and bronchi due to destruction of mucous glands. This is an extension of the sicca syndrome affecting the airways.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sjögren syndrome causes autoimmune destruction of exocrine glands. In the respiratory tract, this leads to xerotrachea (dryness of the tracheal and bronchial mucosa) due to loss of mucous gland function. The resulting dry cough is the most common respiratory symptom and can occur with a normal chest radiograph.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pulmonary fibrosis (ILD) can occur in Sjögren syndrome but would typically cause dyspnea, abnormal PFTs, and radiographic findings, not isolated cough with normal imaging.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pleural effusion is not a typical manifestation of Sjögren syndrome and would be visible on chest radiograph.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bronchogenic carcinoma could cause cough but is not specifically associated with Sjögren syndrome and would likely show an abnormality on chest radiograph.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary embolism presents acutely with dyspnea and chest pain, not chronic dry cough with normal imaging.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man with EGPA (eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis) develops chest pain and dyspnea. Troponin is markedly elevated. ECG shows ST-segment changes. Echocardiography reveals reduced ejection fraction with regional wall motion abnormalities. Which of the following is the most common cause of death in EGPA?

explanation.summary: Cardiac involvement is the most serious complication and the leading cause of death in EGPA. It can manifest as myocarditis, coronary vasculitis, pericarditis, or cardiomyopathy, leading to heart failure, arrhythmias, or sudden death.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cardiac disease is the leading cause of mortality in EGPA. The vasculitic stage causes inflammation of coronary vessels and myocardium, leading to myocardial ischemia, myocarditis, cardiomyopathy, and potentially fatal arrhythmias. Elevated troponin and wall motion abnormalities indicate myocardial damage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Renal failure can occur in EGPA from vasculitis-related glomerulonephritis but is not the most common cause of death.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonary hemorrhage is more associated with GPA and anti-GBM disease. While EGPA affects the lungs, pulmonary hemorrhage is not the leading cause of death.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Stroke can occur from cerebral vasculitis in EGPA but is less common than cardiac-related mortality.
whyWrongByChoice.E: GI perforation from mesenteric vasculitis can occur but is less common than cardiac complications as a cause of death.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis who works as a coal miner presents with dyspnea and multiple well-defined rounded opacities (0.5–2 cm) on chest radiograph bilaterally. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Caplan syndrome (rheumatoid pneumoconiosis) is the combination of rheumatoid arthritis with occupational coal dust (or silica) exposure, producing multiple well-defined rounded pulmonary nodules. It is a specific association between RA and pneumoconiosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Caplan syndrome occurs when a patient with rheumatoid arthritis has concomitant exposure to coal dust or silica. The combination produces characteristic well-defined rounded pulmonary nodules (Caplan nodules) that are larger and develop more rapidly than simple coal workers' pneumoconiosis nodules.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Metastatic disease can cause rounded pulmonary nodules, but the combination of RA and coal mining exposure makes Caplan syndrome far more likely.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sarcoidosis causes bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and reticulonodular infiltrates, not well-defined rounded opacities in a coal miner with RA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Simple silicosis causes small rounded opacities predominantly in the upper lobes, but the RA history and larger nodule size indicate Caplan syndrome specifically.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis shows bilateral ground-glass opacities in a "crazy paving" pattern, not discrete rounded nodules.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with SLE presents with progressive dyspnea and decreasing lung volumes on serial PFTs over 2 years. FVC is 58% predicted. Chest radiograph shows elevated hemidiaphragms bilaterally. HRCT shows no parenchymal abnormalities. Which of the following conditions best explains these findings?

explanation.summary: Shrinking lung syndrome is a rare complication of SLE characterized by progressive restrictive lung disease with elevated hemidiaphragms but no parenchymal lung disease on imaging. It is thought to be caused by diaphragmatic myopathy or pleuritis-related splinting.
explanation.whyCorrect: Shrinking lung syndrome is characterized by progressively decreasing lung volumes, elevated diaphragms, and dyspnea without parenchymal lung disease on imaging. It is specific to SLE and thought to result from diaphragmatic dysfunction. The absence of HRCT abnormalities distinguishes it from ILD or pneumonitis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lupus pneumonitis would show parenchymal infiltrates on imaging and typically presents acutely with fever and hypoxemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pulmonary fibrosis would show honeycombing, reticular changes, or traction bronchiectasis on HRCT, which are absent here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bilateral pleural effusions would be visible on chest radiograph and HRCT, and the diaphragms would be obscured, not elevated.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Phrenic nerve palsy could cause diaphragmatic elevation but would typically be unilateral and does not specifically explain the progressive volume loss pattern seen in this SLE patient.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man presents with hemoptysis, sinusitis, and hematuria. c-ANCA is positive. Anti-PR3 antibody testing is also positive. What is the combined specificity of these two positive tests for granulomatosis with polyangiitis?

explanation.summary: c-ANCA alone is highly specific (>90%) for GPA. When both c-ANCA and anti-PR3 antibodies are positive together, the diagnostic specificity for GPA exceeds 99%.
explanation.whyCorrect: c-ANCA (cytoplasmic ANCA) targets proteinase 3 (PR3) and is seen in most GPA patients. The specificity of c-ANCA alone is >90%. When both c-ANCA and anti-PR3 are confirmed positive, the combined specificity for GPA is greater than 99%, making this a highly reliable serologic diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Greater than 50% significantly underestimates the specificity. Even c-ANCA alone has specificity exceeding 90%.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Greater than 75% also underestimates the combined specificity of c-ANCA plus anti-PR3.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Greater than 90% describes c-ANCA alone. The combination with anti-PR3 raises specificity above 99%.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While the specificity is very high, no serologic test achieves absolute 100% specificity. Tissue biopsy remains the confirmatory test.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with hemoptysis and cola-colored urine. Serum anti-GBM antibodies are positive. Renal biopsy confirms crescentic glomerulonephritis. Which of the following types of hypersensitivity reaction is responsible for the pathology of this disease?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Combined type I and type III

explanation.summary: Anti-GBM disease is a classic example of type II hypersensitivity, in which IgG autoantibodies bind directly to type IV collagen in the glomerular and alveolar basement membranes, activating complement and phagocytes to cause tissue damage.
explanation.whyCorrect: Type II hypersensitivity involves antibodies (IgG or IgM) binding to cell-surface or extracellular matrix antigens. In anti-GBM disease, IgG antibodies bind to type IV collagen in basement membranes, activating complement (C3a, C5a) and recruiting phagocytes that release reactive oxygen species, causing tissue damage.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Type I hypersensitivity involves IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation (e.g., anaphylaxis, allergic asthma), not antibody-mediated tissue destruction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Type III hypersensitivity involves deposition of circulating immune complexes (e.g., SLE nephritis). Anti-GBM disease involves direct antibody binding to tissue antigens, not immune complex deposition.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A combination of type I and III does not apply to anti-GBM disease, which is purely type II.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Type IV hypersensitivity is T-cell mediated (e.g., contact dermatitis, tuberculin reaction). Anti-GBM disease is antibody-mediated.

questions.stem: A 47-year-old man with EGPA is found to have ANCA positivity. Which of the following ANCA patterns and target antigens is most commonly associated with EGPA?

explanation.summary: EGPA, when ANCA-positive, is most commonly associated with p-ANCA (perinuclear pattern) targeting myeloperoxidase (MPO). However, only 30–40% of EGPA patients are ANCA positive, making ANCA testing less useful than in GPA.
explanation.whyCorrect: When ANCA is positive in EGPA (present in 30–40% of cases), it is typically p-ANCA with antibodies directed against myeloperoxidase (MPO-ANCA). This contrasts with GPA, which is associated with c-ANCA targeting proteinase 3.

whyWrongByChoice.A: c-ANCA targeting proteinase 3 (PR3) is characteristic of GPA, not EGPA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: c-ANCA (cytoplasmic pattern) targeting myeloperoxidase is not a recognized combination. MPO staining produces a perinuclear (p-ANCA) pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.D: p-ANCA targeting proteinase 3 is not a recognized combination. PR3 produces a cytoplasmic (c-ANCA) staining pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Atypical ANCA targeting elastase is not a recognized finding in EGPA or other vasculitides.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis develops hoarseness and inspiratory stridor. Direct laryngoscopy reveals swelling and fixation of the cricoarytenoid joints. Which of the following best explains this finding?

explanation.summary: Rheumatoid arthritis can affect the cricoarytenoid joints, which are true synovial joints. Cricoarytenoid arthritis causes hoarseness, stridor, and can even lead to airway obstruction in severe cases. It is a recognized but underdiagnosed extra-articular manifestation of RA.
explanation.whyCorrect: The cricoarytenoid joints are synovial joints susceptible to the same inflammatory arthritis affecting peripheral joints in RA. Inflammation causes swelling and fixation of these joints, leading to vocal cord immobility, hoarseness, and potentially life-threatening stridor.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Recurrent laryngeal nerve compression causes vocal cord paralysis but would show cord immobility without joint swelling, not joint fixation on laryngoscopy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Laryngeal carcinoma would show a mass lesion on laryngoscopy, not bilateral joint swelling and fixation in an RA patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vocal cord polyps cause hoarseness but appear as localized mucosal lesions, not joint inflammation and fixation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Granulomatous infiltration of the larynx occurs in GPA or sarcoidosis, not RA. RA causes synovial joint inflammation, not granulomatous disease.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with SLE and antiphospholipid antibody syndrome presents with acute-onset dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain. Heart rate is 112/min, respiratory rate is 24/min, and oxygen saturation is 89%. CT pulmonary angiography shows a filling defect in the right pulmonary artery. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: SLE patients with antiphospholipid antibody syndrome are at significantly increased risk for thromboembolic events, including pulmonary embolism. A filling defect on CT pulmonary angiography confirms the diagnosis of PE.
explanation.whyCorrect: Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome (APS) creates a hypercoagulable state, placing SLE patients at high risk for venous thromboembolism. The acute presentation with dyspnea, tachycardia, hypoxemia, and a filling defect on CTPA confirms pulmonary embolism.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lupus pneumonitis presents with diffuse infiltrates on imaging, not a filling defect in the pulmonary artery.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Lupus pleuritis causes pleuritic pain and pleural effusion but not a pulmonary arterial filling defect or acute hemodynamic compromise.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage presents with hemoptysis and bilateral alveolar infiltrates, not an arterial filling defect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Shrinking lung syndrome causes progressive dyspnea with elevated diaphragms, not acute onset with an arterial filling defect.

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman with Sjögren syndrome undergoes HRCT for evaluation of progressive dyspnea. The scan shows diffuse bilateral ground-glass opacities with thin-walled cysts scattered throughout the lung parenchyma. Lung biopsy shows dense lymphocytic infiltration of the alveolar septa. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Lymphocytic interstitial pneumonia (LIP) is the most characteristic interstitial lung disease associated with Sjögren syndrome. It presents with ground-glass opacities and thin-walled cysts on HRCT, with dense lymphocytic infiltration of alveolar septa on biopsy.
explanation.whyCorrect: LIP is the most characteristic ILD pattern in Sjögren syndrome. It is characterized by diffuse lymphocytic infiltration of the pulmonary interstitium. HRCT shows ground-glass opacities with thin-walled cysts, and biopsy confirms dense lymphocytic infiltration of the alveolar septa.

whyWrongByChoice.A: UIP shows a pattern of honeycombing with peripheral and basal predominance and fibroblast foci on biopsy, not diffuse lymphocytic infiltration with cysts.
whyWrongByChoice.C: NSIP shows uniform ground-glass opacities without cysts and homogeneous inflammation or fibrosis on biopsy, not dense lymphocytic infiltration with cysts.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Organizing pneumonia shows patchy consolidation and polypoid plugs of granulation tissue in airways, not thin-walled cysts with lymphocytic infiltration.
whyWrongByChoice.B: DIP shows ground-glass opacities and macrophage filling of alveoli, associated with smoking, not lymphocytic infiltration with cysts in Sjögren syndrome.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with GPA (granulomatosis with polyangiitis) has active disease with pulmonary nodules and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?

explanation.summary: Active GPA with organ-threatening disease (pulmonary nodules and RPGN) requires aggressive immunosuppression with corticosteroids combined with either cyclophosphamide or rituximab. Plasmapheresis may be added for severe renal disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: The standard induction therapy for active GPA with major organ involvement is corticosteroids plus either cyclophosphamide or rituximab. This combination rapidly suppresses the autoimmune inflammation causing vasculitis and granulomatous tissue destruction in the lungs and kidneys.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Methotrexate may be used for limited, non–organ-threatening GPA but is insufficient for active disease with RPGN and pulmonary nodules.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Prednisone alone is insufficient for severe GPA. A cytotoxic or biologic immunosuppressant is needed in combination.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Azathioprine and mycophenolate are maintenance agents used after induction of remission, not as initial treatment for active severe disease.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Plasmapheresis alone is insufficient. It may be added to the standard regimen for severe renal disease but does not replace immunosuppressive therapy.

questions.stem: A 48-year-old man with EGPA is started on treatment with corticosteroids. Despite initial improvement, his eosinophilia and asthma symptoms recur when the prednisone dose is tapered. His physician adds an interleukin-5–blocking antibody. Which of the following medications was most likely added?

explanation.summary: Mepolizumab is an anti-interleukin-5 (IL-5) monoclonal antibody approved for treatment of EGPA. IL-5 is the key cytokine driving eosinophil production and activation, making mepolizumab effective in controlling eosinophilia and reducing relapse in EGPA.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mepolizumab binds to IL-5, preventing it from activating eosinophils. It is specifically approved for the treatment of EGPA, helping reduce eosinophilia, control symptoms, and allow corticosteroid dose reduction in patients with relapsing disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Omalizumab is an anti-IgE antibody used for allergic asthma and chronic urticaria. It does not target IL-5 and is not approved for EGPA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dupilumab blocks IL-4 and IL-13 and is used for atopic dermatitis and eosinophilic esophagitis. It is not an IL-5 blocker.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Benralizumab targets the IL-5 receptor alpha and is approved for eosinophilic asthma but is not specifically approved for EGPA. The question specifies an IL-5-blocking antibody, which is mepolizumab.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Infliximab is an anti-TNF-alpha antibody used for inflammatory conditions like Crohn disease and refractory sarcoidosis, not for EGPA.
